message:
chore: close bd-1now.4 telemetry epic and bd-1now.4.8 operator docs

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -204,15 +204,15 @@
 {"id":"bd-1now.3","title":"Add guardrail against ambient Tokio/runtime reintroduction","description":"Why this exists:
`bd-1now.2` removed the dead Tokio bootstrap, but that cleanup will decay unless the repo encodes a concrete anti-regression rule. This cluster turns the runtime-boundary rule into something contributors can see, run, and understand.

What this cluster must do:
- implement a deterministic guardrail against unjustified direct Tokio bootstrap/runtime reintroduction inside `frankenengine-node`,
- make the architectural exception path explicit instead of forcing future contributors to guess how legitimate async-boundary work should be introduced,
- add both unit-level and end-to-end negative fixtures so the guardrail is proved rather than assumed,
- ensure failure output is detailed enough to tell developers exactly what tripped, why it is banned, and what architectural work is required before the ban may be relaxed.

Why this matters:
Without a real checker plus proof harness, ambient executor scaffolding tends to creep back one convenience macro at a time. Users do not benefit from that drift, and future maintainers lose the audit trail that justified the cleanup in the first place.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The repo has a deterministic guardrail that fails when `frankenengine-node` reintroduces unjustified Tokio bootstrap/runtime scaffolding.
2. The exception path is documented and precise: a real async boundary, updated decision record, and an intentionally reviewed relaxation mechanism rather than an ad hoc reintroduction.
3. The guardrail has dedicated proof coverage: unit tests for true-positive / false-positive boundaries, deterministic negative and allowed-case end-to-end fixtures, and reusable machine-readable plus human-readable artifacts.
4. Failure output is specific enough for contributors to act on immediately, including the offending pattern/surface and the policy reason.
5. Any heavy validation needed by this cluster uses `rch` rather than local cargo execution.","notes":"This cluster is intentionally split into implementation and proof. The goal is not “ban Tokio forever”; the goal is “ban ambient executor creep unless the crate truly grows a boundary that warrants it.”

Keep the checker cheap enough to run routinely, but do not trade away precision. False positives that punish legitimate future async-boundary work will cause contributors to route around the guardrail instead of respecting it.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"WindyDesert","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.876029147Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T05:12:07.189726035Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T05:12:07.189696610Z","close_reason":"Verified live guardrail, blueprint exception path, 8 unit tests, and deterministic E2E evidence; parent acceptance satisfied","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","plan","runtime","self-contained","test-obligations","tokio-cleanup","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:40.876029147Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.302404126Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.507463948Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.3.1","title":"Implement deterministic Tokio/bootstrap drift checker and explicit exception path","description":"Why this exists:
The policy against ambient Tokio/runtime reintroduction needs an implementation surface that is narrow, deterministic, and understandable.

What this task must do:
Implement the checker / static guardrail for `frankenengine-node` source surfaces that should not regain ambient executor scaffolding.

The guardrail should focus on the actual regression risk, such as:
- `#[tokio::main]` or equivalent runtime bootstrap attributes,
- direct Tokio runtime builders that recreate a hidden ambient executor shell for the CLI,
- other crate-local executor bootstrap patterns that would reintroduce dead scaffolding without a real async boundary.

Design constraints:
- target source surfaces precisely enough to avoid noisy false positives in docs, fixtures, or intentionally exempted test assets,
- make any explicit exception mechanism impossible to use accidentally,
- keep the rule legible so contributors can understand it from the checker output and source comments.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The checker deterministically flags unjustified Tokio/bootstrap runtime patterns on the intended `frankenengine-node` source surfaces.
2. The checker is precise enough to avoid routine false positives on docs, test fixtures, or explicitly exempted policy-test assets.
3. The checker output identifies the offending file/pattern and points contributors at the architectural exception path.
4. The checker emits stable machine-readable reasoning or rule identifiers plus human-readable remediation guidance so later tests, CI, and humans can all interpret failures consistently.
5. The implementation is cheap enough to run in ordinary validation flows.","notes":"Bias toward explicit pattern checks over fuzzy heuristics. The value here is a small, trustworthy guardrail, not an over-general scanner.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:33:27.614524197Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:53:15.527177621Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T04:53:15.527152274Z","close_reason":"Implemented deterministic tokio/bootstrap guardrail checker with blueprint exception path","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","plan","runtime","self-contained","test-obligations","tokio-cleanup","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:33:27.614524197Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.3.2","title":"Add unit and end-to-end proof harness for Tokio guardrail with detailed failure logs","description":"Why this exists:
A guardrail is only trustworthy if the repo proves both that it catches the bad cases and that its failure path is understandable under real invocation conditions.

What this task must do:
Add the proof harness for the Tokio/bootstrap drift guardrail.

The proof harness should include:
- focused unit tests for true positives, false positives, and any documented exception boundary,
- at least one deterministic negative end-to-end fixture (for example under `tests/e2e/` or an equivalent scripted harness) that injects a forbidden bootstrap pattern and verifies the checker fails as designed,
- detailed failure logging / machine-readable output so a broken validation run explains exactly what happened without manual archaeology.

Why this matters:
This is what turns the guardrail from tribal policy into a regression gate future contributors can trust.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Unit tests cover true-positive matches, false-positive boundaries, and the explicit exception path if one exists.
2. At least one deterministic end-to-end negative fixture demonstrates that reintroducing a forbidden Tokio/bootstrap pattern fails the validation flow, and at least one deterministic allowed-case fixture proves ordinary permitted crate surfaces still pass under repo-like invocation conditions.
3. Failure output is detailed and stable enough for triage, including the offending file/pattern and a clear policy explanation.
4. The proof harness preserves machine-readable summary output plus reusable human-readable logs/artifacts with stable locations so the closeout gate can consume them directly.
5. If the proof harness requires cargo-heavy validation, that validation is executed through `rch`.","notes":"Prefer stable failure prefixes / reason codes for this harness so CI and humans can both recognize the policy violation quickly. The logs should explain how to proceed when the contributor is intentionally introducing a real async boundary.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:33:28.018706117Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:56:35.540127702Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T04:56:35.540091214Z","close_reason":"Added unit tests and deterministic E2E proof harness for tokio bootstrap guardrail","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","e2e","runtime","self-contained","test-obligations","testing","tokio-cleanup","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:33:28.018706117Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.3.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:33:28.413667337Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-1now.4","title":"TelemetryBridge selective Asupersync adoption cluster","description":"Why this exists:
The audit identified the telemetry ingestion path as the one obviously high-value runtime seam in `franken_node`.

Current shape:
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/telemetry_bridge.rs` starts a background listener with `thread::spawn`.
- Each accepted connection spawns another background thread.
- Persistence is shared through `Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>`.
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/engine_dispatcher.rs` starts the bridge as a side effect before dispatching the engine process, but does not surface an explicit stop/join lifecycle handle back to the caller.

Why this is the right selective-adoption target:
This seam has the exact properties Asupersync is strongest at: owned long-lived work, lifecycle obligations, shutdown sequencing, backpressure, deterministic failure reasoning, and operator-visible state transitions. If any runtime area in this crate deserves stronger ownership/supervision semantics, it is this one.

Goal:
Plan and execute a surgical migration of the telemetry listener toward explicit owned-worker/supervised semantics, without forcing a broad crate-wide rewrite.

User-facing / operator-facing outcomes required from this cluster:
- no silent telemetry worker leaks,
- no ambiguous shutdown ordering,
- explicit overflow/backpressure behavior,
- structured lifecycle and failure diagnostics good enough for incident handling,
- deterministic test and artifact coverage so the runtime story is proved, not narrated.

Non-goals:
- Do not use this as a pretext to rewrite unrelated runtime/model modules.
- Do not add Asupersync dependencies casually if a local owned-worker implementation with equivalent semantics is the right fit.
- Do not preserve the existing nested-thread shape just because it currently “works.”","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry ingestion path has an explicit ownership/lifecycle contract rather than incidental background-thread behavior.
2. Work admission, shutdown, error propagation, persistence ownership, operator-visible overflow behavior, budget/tuning policy, event-accounting semantics, and measured performance envelopes are explicit and testable.
3. The cluster includes dedicated proof beads for deterministic unit/integration tests, end-to-end scripts, structured failure-forensics logging, schema/version-stable artifacts, telemetry performance/budget evidence, and an operator-facing troubleshooting/tuning reference.
4. The resulting architecture is justified as a focused, high-value runtime improvement rather than a symbolic migration.
5. Heavy validation for this cluster is explicitly routed through `rch`.","notes":"This cluster is where Asupersync-style structured concurrency / supervision ideas are most likely to pay off immediately. The standard for success is user-visible robustness and diagnosability, not just prettier internal concurrency diagrams.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.273377685Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:29:49.753273473Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","plan","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.273377685Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.708343772Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

The audit identified the telemetry ingestion path as the one obviously high-value runtime seam in `franken_node`.

Current shape:
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/telemetry_bridge.rs` starts a background listener with `thread::spawn`.
- Each accepted connection spawns another background thread.
- Persistence is shared through `Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>`.
- `crates/franken-node/src/ops/engine_dispatcher.rs` starts the bridge as a side effect before dispatching the engine process, but does not surface an explicit stop/join lifecycle handle back to the caller.

Why this is the right selective-adoption target:
This seam has the exact properties Asupersync is strongest at: owned long-lived work, lifecycle obligations, shutdown sequencing, backpressure, deterministic failure reasoning, and operator-visible state transitions. If any runtime area in this crate deserves stronger ownership/supervision semantics, it is this one.

Goal:
Plan and execute a surgical migration of the telemetry listener toward explicit owned-worker/supervised semantics, without forcing a broad crate-wide rewrite.

User-facing / operator-facing outcomes required from this cluster:
- no silent telemetry worker leaks,
- no ambiguous shutdown ordering,
- explicit overflow/backpressure behavior,
- structured lifecycle and failure diagnostics good enough for incident handling,
- deterministic test and artifact coverage so the runtime story is proved, not narrated.

Non-goals:
- Do not use this as a pretext to rewrite unrelated runtime/model modules.
- Do not add Asupersync dependencies casually if a local owned-worker implementation with equivalent semantics is the right fit.
- Do not preserve the existing nested-thread shape just because it currently “works.”","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry ingestion path has an explicit ownership/lifecycle contract rather than incidental background-thread behavior.

4. The resulting architecture is justified as a focused, high-value runtime improvement rather than a symbolic migration.
5. Heavy validation for this cluster is explicitly routed through `rch`.","notes":"This cluster is where Asupersync-style structured concurrency / supervision ideas are most likely to pay off immediately. The standard for success is user-visible robustness and diagnosability, not just prettier internal concurrency diagrams.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.273377685Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T09:32:42.891776452Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T09:32:42.891747778Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","plan","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.273377685Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.708343772Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.4.1","title":"Specify TelemetryBridge ownership, backpressure, and lifecycle contract","description":"Why this exists:
TelemetryBridge should not be refactored by intuition alone. The current path mixes accept-loop ownership, connection handling, persistence ownership, and process lifecycle concerns. A spec-first contract is needed before any implementation churn.

What this task must do:
Define the target ownership/lifecycle contract for telemetry ingestion.

The contract must answer:
- What owns the listener?
- What owns per-connection read loops?
- Whether per-connection concurrency is bounded, serialized, or pooled.
- What owns `FrankensqliteAdapter` writes.
- What the explicit lifecycle handle looks like (`start`, `stop`, `join`, error reporting).
- How backpressure behaves when the engine emits events faster than persistence can absorb them.
- What invariants must hold during engine exit, reader failure, socket cleanup, and persistence failure.
- Whether the implementation should use native Asupersync surfaces directly or a local owned-worker design with equivalent semantics.

Why this matters:
Without this contract, implementation work risks replacing one vague concurrency shape with another.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The design specifies explicit ownership for listener, connection handling, persistence, and shutdown responsibilities.
2. The design specifies a bounded work-admission/backpressure policy and makes overflow behavior an explicit product/operator decision rather than an accident of implementation.
3. The design defines the lifecycle API exposed to `EngineDispatcher`, including stop/join/error semantics and lifecycle-state transitions.
4. The design states clearly whether native Asupersync primitives are required at this seam or whether equivalent local structured semantics are sufficient.
5. The design names the structured event families / required log fields that later tests and E2E scripts must observe (for example bridge identity, queue depth/capacity, lifecycle state, decision, and reason code).
6. The design names event-accounting invariants that preserve user-visible correctness, including what counts as accepted, persisted, dropped/shed, retried, and successfully drained work, plus any ordering guarantees or allowed relaxations.
7. The design names the invariants that the deterministic unit/integration, E2E, and performance suites must later prove.
8. The design names which current/pre-refactor behaviors later performance and closeout evidence must compare against, so the migration cannot claim improvement while silently regressing user-visible throughput, shutdown, or diagnosability.
9. The design states which queue/admission/shutdown budgets are fixed constants, operator-tunable settings, or auto-derived values, and explains why that choice best serves user/operator outcomes.","notes":"2026-03-10 GentleCave: drafted the TelemetryBridge ownership/backpressure/lifecycle contract in docs/migration/asupersync_control_surface_migration.md. The appendix defines the current code-grounded problem (EngineDispatcher side-effect startup, detached listener/per-connection threads, Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>), the target owned-worker topology, bounded admission and shed-on-timeout policy, lifecycle API and state machine, structured event families and reason codes, event-accounting invariants, follow-on proof obligations, baseline comparison targets, and fixed vs tunable vs auto-derived budgets. Heavy cargo validation for follow-on implementation remains explicitly routed through rch.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"GentleCave","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.680480829Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:54:23.945014724Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T04:54:23.944986291Z","close_reason":"Completed: TelemetryBridge ownership/backpressure/lifecycle contract landed in docs/migration/asupersync_control_surface_migration.md","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","design","plan","runtime","self-contained","telemetry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:45.914931602Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:41.680480829Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.4.2","title":"Refactor TelemetryBridge listener and connection handling into owned worker topology","description":"Why this exists:
The accept loop and per-connection handling are currently modeled as nested detached threads. That shape makes lifecycle ownership implicit, complicates shutdown reasoning, and makes it easy to accumulate hidden concurrency without an explicit budget.

What this task must do:
Refactor TelemetryBridge so connection-handling work lives in an explicit owned topology rather than nested incidental threads.

Scope:
- Replace the current listener + nested per-connection thread fan-out with an owned-worker topology chosen in the design task.
- Ensure the topology makes concurrency and ownership explicit.
- Ensure connection cleanup and listener teardown are deterministic and inspectable.
- Avoid a design where accepted work can silently outlive the caller with no handle.

Why this matters:
This is the core structured-concurrency step in the telemetry migration.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The listener and connection-handling topology no longer rely on detached nested `thread::spawn` fan-out with no explicit ownership handle.
2. The chosen topology makes concurrency bounds and ownership explicit.
3. Connection cleanup, listener closure, and terminal states are deterministic enough to support regression testing.
4. The implementation exposes stable observability hooks for later proof harnesses and operators, including lifecycle transitions, accept/close reasons, and enough worker/connection identity to correlate events.
5. The implementation matches the contract chosen by the design bead rather than inventing a new shape ad hoc.","notes":"This task is about work topology, not persistence ownership. Keep the queue/DB-owner decision separate so failures can be reasoned about cleanly.

Do not hide the new topology behind opaque helper layers that make tests and operator logs harder to interpret. The implementation should make worker ownership and state transitions more legible, not less.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.101522618Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T07:42:37.758993439Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T07:42:37.758896108Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","implementation","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.101522618Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.117375530Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.4.3","title":"Replace telemetry shared-mutex persistence with bounded single-owner pipeline","description":"Why this exists:
The current telemetry path shares persistence through `Arc<Mutex<FrankensqliteAdapter>>`. That is simple, but it leaves ownership ambiguous and hides throughput/lock-contention/backpressure semantics behind a mutex.

What this task must do:
Replace the shared-mutex persistence path with an explicit bounded ingestion/persistence pipeline whose ownership and overflow behavior are deliberate.

Scope:
- Give one clearly defined worker/component ownership of persistence writes.
- Introduce an explicit bounded queue or equivalent admission-control mechanism between readers and persistence.
- Define what happens when persistence cannot keep up: block, shed, fail, or batch — but make it explicit.
- Preserve deterministic replay/audit correctness requirements while improving ownership clarity.

Why this matters:
Single-owner persistence is closer to Asupersync’s actor/obligation mindset and is much easier to reason about under failure than a shared mutex in a multi-threaded fan-out path.","acceptance_criteria":"1. `FrankensqliteAdapter` write ownership is explicit and no longer hidden behind a broadly shared `Arc<Mutex<...>>` in the telemetry ingestion hot path.
2. Ingestion uses bounded admission or an equally explicit alternative, with documented overflow/backpressure policy.
3. Persistence failure semantics are explicit and testable.
4. Overflow/backpressure and persistence decisions are surfaced through stable diagnostics/events required by later tests and operator triage, such as queue depth/capacity, decision, and reason code.
5. The pipeline exposes stable accounting signals for accepted, persisted, retried, drained, and intentionally dropped work so later proof lanes can detect silent loss or duplication.
6. The change preserves audit/replay integrity while improving lifecycle clarity and hands measurable budget inputs to the later performance-evidence bead.","notes":"Do not choose an unbounded queue here unless the design task justifies why that is safer than explicit backpressure. Unbounded ingestion would erase one of the main advantages of doing this work.

Treat operator-facing visibility as part of the design, not a post-hoc debugging aid. If the system sheds, blocks, batches, or fails, users need evidence that explains which path occurred.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.517609133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T07:48:33.793717829Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T07:48:33.793691159Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","persistence","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.517609133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.319640615Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":180,"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.3","author":"JadeCave","text":"Implemented the bounded single-owner telemetry persistence lane in crates/franken-node/src/ops/telemetry_bridge.rs: explicit sync_channel admission, single persistence-owner worker, stable counters/snapshot/event surface, and tests for queue-shed/disconnect outcomes. A targeted remote rustfmt --check on this file passes. Workspace cargo validation remains blocked by unrelated upstream errors in sibling franken_engine files (currently resource_certificate_consumer.rs derive failures for ExtensionBudgetState Eq/PartialEq, and earlier formatter blockers in sibling repos), so I am keeping bd-1now.4.3 in_progress until the tree is green enough to rerun cargo check/test cleanly.","created_at":"2026-03-10T05:16:31Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.4.4","title":"Wire EngineDispatcher to explicit telemetry lifecycle, shutdown, and error propagation","description":"Why this exists:
`EngineDispatcher` currently starts telemetry ingestion as a background side effect before launching the engine process. The caller does not receive an explicit lifecycle object describing how the bridge is stopped, joined, observed for failure, or surfaced to operators.

What this task must do:
Wire `EngineDispatcher` to an explicit telemetry lifecycle handle and lifecycle-state model.

Scope:
- Starting telemetry must return or retain a concrete lifecycle handle/state object.
- Engine dispatch must define what happens to telemetry workers when engine startup fails, engine exit is normal, engine exit is abnormal, or telemetry itself fails.
- Socket cleanup must be deterministic and not depend on lucky process teardown ordering.
- Lifecycle transitions and failure paths must emit stable reasoned states / diagnostics that later tests and E2E scripts can assert.
- Any stop/join/error path must match the contract from the design task.

Why this matters:
Even a perfectly designed worker topology is incomplete if the main dispatch path still treats it as an unmanaged side effect.","acceptance_criteria":"1. `EngineDispatcher` owns and resolves telemetry lifecycle explicitly rather than starting background work and forgetting it.
2. Startup-failure, engine-exit, and telemetry-failure paths are all defined and implemented.
3. Socket cleanup and worker shutdown are deterministic and observable through stable lifecycle states / reasoned diagnostics.
4. The dispatch path emits stable operator-facing state transitions and reason codes so tests, logs, and incident review can reconstruct why telemetry started, stopped, drained, or failed.
5. The resulting dispatch path makes it possible to reason about “who is still running?” and “why did it stop?” without guesswork.","notes":"This is the obligation-resolution bead for the telemetry cluster. Treat the lifecycle handle as something that must be resolved deliberately, not dropped casually.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.974073391Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T08:30:30.556383674Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T08:30:30.556355892Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","dispatcher","runtime","telemetry","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:42.974073391Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:46.120597045Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.518698934Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.751289971Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.4.5","title":"Add deterministic telemetry unit/integration regression suite with failure-forensics logging","description":"Why this exists:
A runtime-ownership refactor only pays off if the repo can prove the new lifecycle semantics under load, failure, and shutdown. Otherwise the code merely looks more structured while hiding the same bugs.

What this task must do:
Add a deterministic unit/integration regression suite for telemetry ingestion lifecycle and failure behavior.

The suite should prove, at minimum:
- listener startup and shutdown sequencing,
- socket cleanup after normal and abnormal engine termination,
- bounded/backpressure behavior under event bursts,
- persistence failure handling,
- no orphan workers or silently abandoned ingestion state after lifecycle resolution,
- stable structured logs / artifacts sufficient to diagnose failures later.

Test style requirements:
- prefer deterministic clocks, fakes, and fault injection over flaky timing races,
- assert lifecycle-state transitions and required structured event fields,
- keep this bead focused on unit/integration proof; full end-to-end orchestration scripts live in the dedicated E2E bead.

Why this matters:
This is the evidence bead that converts the telemetry migration from a stylistic refactor into a correctness/reliability improvement.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry cluster has deterministic unit/integration tests covering startup, shutdown, abnormal termination, backpressure/overflow, and persistence failure.
2. The tests prove that no owned worker silently outlives its lifecycle boundary after stop/join completes.
3. The tests assert stable structured event families / fields needed for later forensics, such as lifecycle state, reason code, queue depth/capacity, and worker identity.
4. The tests explicitly verify event-accounting invariants under normal and stressed conditions, including expected accepted/persisted/drained/dropped counts and the absence of silent duplication outside the documented policy.
5. The suite captures detailed logs/artifacts that are directly reusable by the later E2E, performance-evidence, and operator-runbook beads rather than inventing separate ad hoc instrumentation.
6. The suite is reliable enough to act as a regression gate for future lifecycle changes.
7. Cargo-heavy execution for this suite is routed through `rch`.","notes":"If native Asupersync lab/runtime surfaces are not introduced directly here, the tests should still aim for deterministic control over clocks, inputs, and shutdown order. Prefer failure logs that remain legible to operators, not only to the original implementer.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.403136908Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T08:54:11.428153084Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T08:54:11.428127948Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","integration","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.403136908Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:46.949398199Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.4.6","title":"Add telemetry end-to-end orchestration scripts and artifact-rich lifecycle logs","description":"Why this exists:
The telemetry refactor changes long-lived runtime behavior that users and operators will experience indirectly through startup/shutdown behavior, missing/persisted events, and diagnosability under failure. A deterministic end-to-end harness is required in addition to unit/integration tests.

What this task must do:
Add end-to-end telemetry orchestration scripts plus artifact-rich logging for the selective Asupersync telemetry cluster.

The E2E harness should exercise representative operator scenarios such as:
- normal engine startup, telemetry ingestion, and orderly shutdown,
- abnormal engine termination / crash while telemetry is active,
- burst traffic that hits the declared backpressure / overflow policy,
- persistence stall or failure injection,
- socket cleanup and worker-resolution behavior after stop/join completes.

Required outputs:
- at least one deterministic script under `tests/e2e/` (or a tightly justified equivalent harness) for the telemetry cluster,
- machine-readable summary output and human-readable detailed logs,
- structured lifecycle / failure events with stable fields that align to the design contract,
- artifact locations future contributors can reuse when diagnosing regressions.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry cluster has deterministic end-to-end orchestration coverage for normal operation, abnormal termination, backpressure/overflow, persistence failure/stall, and orderly cleanup.
2. The E2E harness emits both machine-readable summaries and human-readable detailed logs/artifacts.
3. The logs contain stable structured fields sufficient for triage, including lifecycle state, reason code, queue state, worker/socket identity, and enough timing context to support later performance characterization.
4. The machine-readable summaries include stable scenario identifiers plus event-accounting receipts, such as expected vs actual accepted/persisted/drained/dropped totals and any policy-authorized loss/shed counts.
5. Artifact locations and schemas are stable and versioned enough that future contributors, the closeout gate, and operator documentation can reuse them directly.
6. Any cargo-heavy build/test work needed for this harness is executed through `rch`.","notes":"The E2E goal is not synthetic coverage for its own sake. It is to prove the operator experience: what happens when the engine exits badly, when the bridge backs up, and what evidence remains afterward.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:46.565647748Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T09:06:51.388046595Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T09:06:51.388010688Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","e2e","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations","testing","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:46.565647748Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:46.976165895Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.4.7","title":"Characterize telemetry throughput, backpressure budgets, and operator-facing performance evidence","description":"Why this exists:
The telemetry refactor is justified partly on robustness and correctness, but users also care whether the new ownership model preserves or improves throughput, burst handling, and drain behavior. Without explicit performance evidence, the project could “clean up” the runtime story while silently regressing real operator experience.

What this task must do:
Characterize the refactored telemetry path under realistic load and backpressure conditions, then publish operator-usable performance evidence.

The characterization should cover at minimum:
- steady-state ingestion behavior,
- burst behavior up to and beyond the declared queue/admission budget,
- drain/shutdown time after stop/join,
- queue depth/capacity evolution,
- enqueue/dequeue / persist latency signals,
- any shed/block/batch/fail behavior that the design selected.

Required outputs:
- repeatable load/performance harnesses using the implemented telemetry scripts/logging,
- machine-readable summaries,
- human-readable operator notes explaining what the measured budgets mean in practice,
- stable artifact names that the closeout gate can reference.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry cluster has repeatable performance/load evidence covering steady-state, burst, overflow/backpressure, and drain/shutdown behavior.
2. The evidence is produced with `rch`-offloaded heavy execution rather than local cargo runs.
3. Machine-readable summaries capture the key user-visible signals: queue depth/capacity, latency or service-time indicators, overflow decisions, drain time, and event-accounting totals needed to interpret throughput without hiding loss; those summaries also carry stable schema/version markers plus scenario identifiers.
4. Human-readable operator notes explain the practical meaning of the measured budgets/thresholds, tradeoffs, and any divergence between ideal throughput and durable persistence guarantees, including any before/after change versus the pre-refactor baseline or clearly declared predecessor behavior; if any budgets are configurable, the notes also explain recommended tuning ranges and consequences, and if they are intentionally fixed, the notes explain why.
5. If the measurements materially contradict the intended design envelope, the bead remains open until the deviation is explained or the implementation/design is corrected.","notes":"This is not a benchmark vanity bead. It exists so the refactor has explicit evidence that it did not trade correctness improvements for unacceptable user-visible throughput or shutdown regressions.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:37:46.303723986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T09:31:07.651538735Z","closed_at":"2026-03-10T09:31:07.651510212Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","performance","rch","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations","testing","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:37:46.303723986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:37:46.697921814Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-1now.4.8","title":"Publish telemetry operator troubleshooting guide and reason-code reference","description":"Why this exists:
Detailed logs and machine-readable summaries only improve user/operator experience if humans can quickly interpret them during incidents or regression triage. The telemetry refactor therefore needs an explicit operator-facing reference aligned to the new states, reason codes, and artifact bundles.

What this task must do:
Publish an operator-facing telemetry troubleshooting/reference guide aligned to the refactored telemetry lifecycle.

The guide should cover at minimum:
- lifecycle states and what they mean,
- overflow/backpressure decision codes and expected operator responses,
- accepted/persisted/drained/dropped accounting fields and how to read them,
- where the E2E, performance, and verification artifacts live,
- common failure modes and the first diagnostic steps to take.

Required outputs:
- a stable docs surface in the repo,
- references that match the actual structured fields and reason codes emitted by the implementation and proof harnesses,
- concise troubleshooting steps that future operators/contributors can follow without reconstructing the architecture from scratch.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The telemetry cluster has an operator-facing reference that maps lifecycle states, reason codes, accounting fields, relevant fixed-vs-configurable budgets/tuning knobs, and artifact locations to concrete interpretation/action.
2. The reference is aligned to the actual machine-readable fields and logs emitted by the proof harnesses rather than aspirational names.
3. The guide covers normal, degraded, and failure scenarios, including overflow/backpressure and abnormal termination.
4. The guide explains how to distinguish policy-authorized shedding, delayed durability, or bounded drain behavior from unexpected loss, duplication, or stuck shutdown using accounting fields, reason codes, and performance-envelope artifacts.
5. The closeout gate can point to this reference as part of the reusable evidence pack for future maintainers/operators.","notes":"This is not generic documentation polish. It is part of the user-facing robustness story: logs are only valuable if operators can decode them quickly under pressure.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:42:37.818221975Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T09:31:21.108552437Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","docs","runtime","self-contained","telemetry","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:42:37.818221975Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:42:38.225874797Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.4.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.4.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:42:38.426406261Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1now.5","title":"Decide local semantic twin policy versus canonical franken_engine/asupersync adapters","description":"Why this exists:
`franken_node` contains local semantic twins of several Asupersync-flavored concepts while `frankenengine-engine` already centralizes canonical upstream primitive imports. If the repo does not define that boundary explicitly, future contributors will oscillate between duplicate-type sprawl and premature migration churn.

What this cluster must do:
- inventory the local semantic twins and classify each one against the canonical upstream ownership boundary,
- explain why each surface should stay local, wrap canonical primitives, migrate only when a live runtime boundary exists, or be forbidden as a duplicate fork,
- add anti-drift proof obligations so the policy remains actionable instead of decaying into prose.

Why this matters:
This is where the project decides what belongs locally for modeling/spec reasons versus what should ultimately collapse onto upstream-owned primitives. The output must be self-contained enough that future maintainers can make consistent decisions without reopening this chat.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The repo has an explicit inventory of local Asupersync-like semantic twins in `franken_node`.
2. Each inventoried surface has a clear policy outcome: keep local, wrap canonical, migrate later under trigger conditions, or forbid as a duplicate primitive fork.
3. The cluster includes anti-drift safeguards, repository-level validation scripts, detailed versioned logging/artifacts, and explicit mapping from policy entries to enforcement/proof surfaces so the policy is enforceable rather than purely narrative.
4. The policy preserves the existing upstream ownership boundary in `frankenengine-engine` and gives future contributors a reliable decision procedure for new concepts, including how to find the rule/check/script that enforces or exemplifies the chosen classification.
5. Any heavy validation needed by this cluster is routed through `rch`.","notes":"This is a semantic-boundary cluster, not a mandate to rewrite everything. A successful outcome may explicitly keep several node-side surfaces local. The important thing is to make those decisions principled, testable where feasible, and reusable by future contributors.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.827371396Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:29:50.618078304Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","boundaries","plan","runtime","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:43.827371396Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:46:47.157217855Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.5.1","title":"Inventory and classify node-side semantic twins against canonical upstream boundaries","description":"Why this exists:
Before the repo can defend a boundary policy, it needs an explicit inventory and classification matrix for the local semantic twins already present.

What this task must do:
Inventory the relevant node-side semantic twins and classify each one against canonical upstream ownership.

The inventory must cover at least:
- `connector/region_ownership.rs`,
- `connector/supervision.rs`,
- `runtime/region_tree.rs`,
- performance budget guards,
- any additional node-side semantic twin discovered while implementing the matrix.

For each surface, capture:
- what problem it solves locally,
- which upstream canonical primitive or adapter it relates to (if any),
- whether it should stay local, wrap canonical ownership, migrate only under a future trigger, or be treated as forbidden duplicate/fork surface,
- what user/operator consequence exists if the surface drifts semantically.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The inventory/classification matrix covers the known node-side semantic twins and any additional relevant surfaces found during implementation.
2. Each entry contains a concrete policy outcome plus the reasoning behind it.
3. Each entry records the user/operator or contributor-facing consequence of semantic drift so the classification is grounded in behavior rather than aesthetics.
4. The matrix includes representative decision examples future contributors can analogize from, including at least one “keep local” case and one “must not fork / should align upstream” case.
5. The matrix is self-contained enough that future contributors can classify a new similar surface by analogy instead of improvising.
6. Each matrix entry points to the intended enforcement/proof surface (checker, test, script, or an explicit justification for why the entry is document-only for now) so the policy is operational rather than merely classificatory.","notes":"Bias toward explicit reasoning over short labels. The goal is for future maintainers to understand why a surface stays local or moves upstream, not just to see a terse classification word.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"GentleCave","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:47.431555934Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-10T04:55:23.222374269Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","boundaries","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:47.431555934Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1now.5.2","title":"Add anti-drift safeguards, examples, and consistency checks for semantic-boundary policy","description":"Why this exists:
A classification matrix without anti-drift proof obligations will decay as soon as new local helpers, wrappers, or canonical imports get added under delivery pressure.

What this task must do:
Add the anti-drift layer for the semantic-boundary policy.

Depending on the surfaces involved, that anti-drift layer may include:
- deterministic unit tests or snapshot tests that protect key local-vs-canonical classification tables,
- examples / contributor-facing decision cases that show when a new concept stays local versus when it must reference canonical upstream ownership,
- cheap checkers that fail when a forbidden duplicate/fork pattern or undocumented boundary crossing reappears,
- stable failure logging or reason codes so policy violations are easy to triage.

Why this matters:
This is what makes the semantic-boundary policy reusable and enforceable rather than a one-time document.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The semantic-boundary policy has deterministic anti-drift safeguards appropriate to the actual surfaces involved (tests, snapshots, examples, checkers, or a justified combination).
2. The safeguards make it obvious when a contributor is creating an undocumented duplicate fork or boundary crossing.
3. Failure output is detailed enough for contributors to understand what changed and which policy rule they violated.
4. The safeguards export stable rule ids/messages and, where applicable, machine-readable fields that the repo-level proof harness can reuse instead of inventing a second policy vocabulary.
5. This bead delivers the implementation-layer safeguards; the repo-level scripted/E2E proof lane is tracked separately.
6. If cargo-heavy validation is needed, it is executed through `rch`.","notes":"Do not overfit this to one current file layout. The anti-drift layer should help future contributors make correct ownership decisions even if files move or additional local semantic surfaces appear.

Keep the implementation-layer checks composable so the later repo-level proof harness can reuse the same rule ids, messages, and artifacts instead of inventing parallel policy logic.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:47.853428848Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-09T22:47:20.828695255Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync","boundaries","self-contained","test-obligations","testing","verification-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:47.853428848Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1now.5.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1now.5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T22:32:48.264057922Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
